results.description: Chromecast was a line of digital media players that allowed users to play online content on a television.

score.why_could_work_now: Chromecast continues to evolve and thrive as a product line, not a failed product.

results.description: YouTube Stories (originally YouTube Reels) allowed creators to post temporary videos that would expire after seven days.

score.why_could_work_now: The short-form vertical video market is saturated, and Stories offered little differentiation to attract creators or viewers away from existing platforms.

results.description: Google Album Archive was a platform that allowed users to access and manage their archived photos and videos from various Google services, such as Hangouts and Picasa Web Albums.

score.why_could_work_now: A dedicated, privacy-focused platform for long-term photo archiving and sharing, with robust search and organization features, is still in demand.

results.description: Google Domains was a domain name registrar operated by Google.

score.why_could_work_now: Many small businesses and individuals value simplicity and reliability in domain registration, and Google's brand could still command trust for a no-frills, integrated service.

results.description: Jacquard was a small tag to make it easier and more intuitive for people to interact with technology in their everyday lives, without having to constantly pull out their devices or touch screens.

score.why_could_work_now: Integration with smart home devices and a focus on modular, easily upgradeable components could address prior limitations.

results.description: Google Surveys was a business product by Google aimed at facilitating customized market research.

score.why_could_work_now: Modern AI could automate survey creation, targeting, and analysis, making it significantly more efficient and insightful for users.

results.description: Google Code Jam, Kick Start, and Hash Code were competitive programming competitions open to programmers around the world.

score.why_could_work_now: The competitive programming landscape is well-served by established platforms.

results.description: Google Duo was a video calling app that allowed people to call someone from their contact list.

score.why_could_work_now: The core problem of simple, high-quality video calls is well-served by existing market leaders, offering little room for a new entrant without significant differentiation.

results.description: Android Auto for phone screens was an app that allowed the screen of the phone to be used as an Android Auto interface while driving, intended for vehicles that did not have a compatible screen built in.

score.why_could_work_now: A dedicated phone-based driving mode with integrated navigation, communication, and media controls could cater to users without in-car infotainment systems.

results.description: Google My Business was an app that allowed businesses to manage their Google Maps Business profiles.

score.why_could_work_now: Local businesses still need a dedicated and intuitive platform for managing their online presence without being buried in Google Maps' complexity.

results.description: YouTube Go was an app aimed at making YouTube easier to access on mobile devices in emerging markets through special features like downloading video on wifi for viewing later.

score.why_could_work_now: A modern, data-lite YouTube version could thrive in regions with expensive data or limited connectivity, especially with improved offline capabilities and creator monetization.

results.description: Google Contributor was a program run by Google that allowed users in the Google Network of content sites to view the websites without any advertisements that are administered, sorted, and maintained by Google.

score.why_could_work_now: The rise of AI-generated content and the increasing desire for ad-free experiences could make a micropayment system for content more attractive.

results.description: Google Maps Coordinate was a service for managing mobile workforces with the help of mobile apps and a web-based dashboard.

score.why_could_work_now: Modern real-time collaboration tools and the increasing need for precise location data in field operations could make a dedicated, enterprise-focused coordinate sharing platform valuable.

results.description: Fitbit Coach (formerly Fitstar) was a video-based bodyweight workout app that used AI to personalize workouts based on user feedback.

score.why_could_work_now: The demand for at-home personalized fitness coaching has surged, with advancements in AI making highly customized and adaptive programs more accessible and affordable than ever.

results.description: Fitstar Yoga was a video-based yoga app that created unique yoga sessions based on user preference and skill level.

score.why_could_work_now: Modern generative AI can create highly personalized and interactive yoga flows, offering unique selling propositions beyond basic video libraries.

results.description: Google OnHub was a series of residential wireless routers manufactured by Asus and TP-Link that were powered by Google software, managed by Google apps, and offered enhanced special features like Google Assistant.

score.why_could_work_now: The original OnHub was ahead of its time for user-friendly home networking, but current market saturation with robust mesh Wi-Fi systems diminishes its unique appeal.

results.description: Analytics platform for Google's Dialogflow chatbot & others, started by the Google-funded Area120 incubator then retired and partially merged into Dialogflow itself.

score.why_could_work_now: The current generative AI landscape makes automated chatbot analysis and optimization more crucial than ever for businesses leveraging AI-powered customer service.
